content:
We label the curvatures (signed) of the circles by k.  The line A has k = 0, the big circle B (radius R=2008) has k_B=1/2008, and the first small circle C₁ has k₁=1.  By Descartes’ theorem, if k_{n–1}, k_B, 0 are three mutually tangent curvatures, then the other solution k_n satisfies

 k_n = k_{n-1} + k_B  –  2√(k_{n-1}k_B)
    = (√k_{n-1} – 1/√2008)².

Hence if we set y_n=√{k_n} we get the simple linear recursion

 y₁ =1, y_n = y_{n–1} – 1/√2008,

so

 y_n = 1 – (n–1)/√2008.

We must have y_n>0 in order to get a genuine (finite‐radius) circle.  Thus

 1 – (n–1)/√2008 > 0
 ⇔ n–1 < √2008
 ⇔ n < 1 + √2008 ≈ 45.82
 ⇔ n ≤ 45.

For n=46 one would get y₄₆≤0, i.e. zero or negative “curvature,” which no longer gives a proper circle.  Therefore the maximal n is

 \boxed{45}.

Hence if we set y_n=√{k_n} we get the simple linear recursion

 y₁ =1, y_n = y_{n–1} – 1/√2008,

so

 y_n = 1 – (n–1)/√2008.

We must have y_n>0 in order to get a genuine (finite‐radius) circle.  Thus

 \boxed{45}.
